title: Welding Processes and Discontinuities
narrationScript: A weld is a small casting made under terrible conditions. It is melted, shielded, and frozen in seconds, in position, between cold walls, and the process has to carry its own atmosphere with it. Every welding process solves those problems differently, and every solution leaves its own fingerprints. That is why a radiographer studies welding. When you know how the joint was made, you know which discontinuities are likely, where they will sit, and what they will look like, before the film ever comes out of the processor. Interpretation without process knowledge is guessing. Start with the flux shielded processes. Shielded metal arc welding, stick welding, melts a flux covered electrode. The covering burns to make shielding gas, lays a protective slag blanket over the bead, and can add alloy. Slag is the price of that protection. If the welder fails to clean between passes, or the bead profile traps it, slag stays in the weld and images as dark irregular lines and spots. The classic pattern is wagon tracks, two parallel dark lines riding the groove faces where slag wedged at the toes of a pass. Stick also brings porosity from damp electrodes or a long arc, and low hydrogen electrodes have to live in heated ovens, because moisture in the covering becomes hydrogen cracking hours after the weld cools. Downhill cellulosic root passes on pipelines have their own signature, hollow bead, an elongated porosity that runs along the root centerline. Submerged arc welding is the other flux process. A bare wire feeds continuously under a blanket of granular flux, no visible arc, very high current and deposition, flat position production work like vessel seams and pipe mills. Set right, it is exceptionally clean. When it goes wrong, it goes wrong in long regular ways. Elongated slag lines along the fusion boundaries, porosity from damp or dirty flux and rusty plate, and centerline cracking when a deep narrow bead freezes from both walls toward the middle. Now the gas shielded wire processes. Gas metal arc welding feeds a solid wire through a gun inside a shielding gas envelope. No flux, so no true slag, just small glassy silicon islands at most. Its character depends on transfer mode. Spray transfer is hot and smooth. Short circuiting transfer is cold and goes all positions, and it is the notorious source of lack of fusion, the cold lap, where the puddle rolls against a sidewall without melting it and freezes there, leaving a smooth straight dark line along the fusion face. Wind is the other enemy. A crosswind of more than about five miles per hour strips the gas shield and fills the weld with porosity. Pipe roots can also show whiskers, stubs of wire that poked through the root and image as short light lines inside the bore, because they add metal to the beam path. Flux cored arc welding looks like the same gun, but the wire is a tube filled with flux, so it deposits like wire and shields like stick. That brings slag back, with all the inclusion risks between passes, and it adds one signature of its own, worm tracks, elongated gas grooves under the slag from moisture in the core. Gas tungsten arc welding is the precision process. A nonconsumable tungsten electrode makes the arc, inert gas protects the puddle, and filler is added separately. It makes the cleanest welds we shoot, which is why critical root passes, thin wall tube, stainless, and titanium go to it. Its fingerprints are unique. Dip the tungsten in the puddle and a fragment stays behind, imaging as a sharp light spot, because tungsten is far denser than steel and absorbs more of the beam. Lose the back purge on stainless and the root oxidizes into a granular sugared surface. There is no slag, so be suspicious of any slag call on a TIG weld. Resistance welding makes nuggets, not beads. Current and electrode force fuse two sheets at their interface, no filler, no flux. Porosity, shrinkage voids, cracks, and expulsion in the nugget will show on a radiograph of thin sheet. But the failure that matters most, the stuck or cold weld where the sheets never truly fused, is a tight interface with no thickness change, and radiography is the wrong tool to find it. The special processes round out the list. Electron beam welding fires a focused beam, usually in vacuum, and melts a deep narrow keyhole, joints up to thirty times deeper than they are wide. Misalign that narrow beam and you can miss the joint entirely, and partial penetration electron beam welds suffer spiking, root voids at the tips of the spikes. Electroslag and electrogas welding fill very thick vertical joints in one pass between water cooled shoes, electroslag by resistance heating in a molten slag bath, electrogas with an arc. Both cool slowly, grow coarse grain, and risk centerline cracking, porosity, and trouble at restarts. Laser welds behave like small electron beam welds. Learn the process first. The film makes sense after that.
imagePrompt: A pipeline welder kneels at a beveled steel pipe joint on wooden skids at dusk, the stick-welding arc flaring blue-white against his helmet, while two technicians stage a gamma radiography exposure device and collimator a short distance down the right-of-way.

sections.heading: Why the Radiographer Studies Welding
sections.body:
Most production radiography is weld radiography, and every weld is a small casting - melted, shielded, and solidified in seconds, in position, between cold walls. Each welding process is a different answer to the same three questions: where does the heat come from, what protects the molten pool from the air, and how is filler metal added. Those answers determine which discontinuities the process is prone to, where they sit in the joint, and what they look like on film.

Slag inclusions exist only where there is flux to make slag. Tungsten inclusions exist only where there is a tungsten electrode. Lack of sidewall fusion haunts low-heat wire transfer; centerline cracking haunts deep, slow-freezing single-pass beads. An interpreter who reads the **welding procedure specification (WPS)** before picking up the film already holds the suspect list; one who does not is pattern-matching in the dark. This lesson walks through the processes a Level II meets - SMAW, SAW, GMAW, FCAW, GTAW, resistance welding, and the special deep-section processes - and pairs each with its signature discontinuities.

sections.caption: How the four principal arc processes carry their heat, filler, and shielding - the features that determine each one's discontinuity list.

sections.heading: The Flux Processes: SMAW and SAW
sections.body:
**Shielded metal arc welding (SMAW)** - stick welding - melts a flux-covered electrode: the covering burns to generate shielding gas, deposits a protective **slag** blanket, stabilizes the arc, and can add alloy. It is manual, all-position, simple, and everywhere in field work. Its discontinuities follow from the flux and the human hand: **slag inclusions** where interpass cleaning failed or the bead profile trapped slag - classically **wagon tracks**, twin dark lines riding the groove faces; **porosity** from damp electrodes, excessive arc length, or contaminated bevels; **undercut**, incomplete penetration, and lack of fusion from technique; **arc strikes** beside the weld; and delayed **hydrogen cracking** when low-hydrogen electrodes were not stored in heated ovens. Downhill cellulosic root passes on pipelines add **hollow bead** - elongated porosity along the root centerline.

**Submerged arc welding (SAW)** buries a continuously fed bare wire under a blanket of granular flux: no visible arc, very high current and deposition, restricted to flat and horizontal work - vessel long seams, pipe-mill double-submerged-arc welds, heavy structurals. Set correctly it is exceptionally clean and uniform. When it goes wrong, it goes wrong in long, regular ways: **elongated slag lines** along the fusion boundaries, **porosity** from damp or contaminated flux, rust, or mill scale, lack of fusion from wire mistracking, and **centerline solidification cracking** when a deep, narrow bead freezes from both walls toward the middle.

sections.heading: The Gas-Shielded Wire Processes: GMAW and FCAW
sections.body:
**Gas metal arc welding (GMAW)** feeds a solid wire through a gun inside an externally supplied shielding-gas envelope - no flux, so no true slag, only small glassy **silica islands**. Its character changes with **transfer mode**: spray transfer is hot, smooth, and flat-position; **short-circuiting transfer (GMAW-S)** is cold and all-position - and is the notorious source of **lack of fusion (cold lap)**, where the puddle rolls against an unmelted sidewall and freezes without union, leaving a smooth, straight dark line along the fusion face. Because the shield is a gas column, **wind** above roughly five miles per hour strips it away and fills the bead with **porosity** - the classic field-weld outbreak. Pipe roots add **whiskers**: wire stubs that poked through the root and image as short **light** lines inside the bore, because they add metal to the beam path. Burn-through and crater cracks complete the list.

**Flux-cored arc welding (FCAW)** runs the same gun with a tubular wire whose core carries flux: it deposits like wire and shields like stick - so it makes **slag**, with all of SMAW's inclusion risks between passes. Gas-shielded FCAW shares GMAW's wind sensitivity; self-shielded FCAW tolerates weather but picks up nitrogen **porosity** at excessive voltage or wrong electrode extension. Its signature artifact is **worm tracks** - elongated gas grooves under the slag from moisture in the core, imaging as wandering dark surface lines.

sections.heading: GTAW: The Precision Process
sections.body:
**Gas tungsten arc welding (GTAW / TIG)** strikes the arc from a **nonconsumable tungsten electrode** inside an inert-gas cup; filler metal, when used, is fed separately into the pool. It is slow and low-deposition but the cleanest, most controllable arc process - which is why critical root passes, thin-wall tube, stainless, nickel, and titanium work, and orbital machine welding belong to it.

Its discontinuities are few and specific: **tungsten inclusions** when the electrode touches the pool or carries too much current and sheds fragments - imaging as sharp **light** spots, because tungsten is roughly two and a half times as dense as steel and absorbs more of the beam; **porosity** from lost gas coverage, drafts, or contaminated filler and base metal; **lack of penetration** when heat input is too low; an oxidized, granular root surface (**sugaring**) on stainless when the inert back purge was lost; and **crater pits and cracks** where the arc was broken without downslope or crater fill. There is no flux and no slag - a 'slag' call on a GTAW film should send the interpreter back for a second look.

sections.caption: Simulated radiograph signatures: a light tungsten inclusion, wagon-track slag lines, straight sidewall lack of fusion, and shield-loss porosity.

sections.heading: Resistance and Special Processes
sections.body:
**Resistance welding** - spot, seam, projection, and flash/upset butt - generates heat from electrical resistance at the faying surfaces under electrode force, with no arc, no filler, and no flux. Fusion occurs as an internal **nugget** between the sheets. Thin-section radiography (an aerospace practice) shows nugget **porosity, shrinkage voids, cracks, and expulsion** - but the most dangerous failure, the **stuck or cold weld** where the surfaces never fused, is a tight interface with no thickness change and is essentially invisible to RT. Bond quality is ultrasonic and mechanical-test territory.

**Electron beam welding (EBW)** focuses a high-energy beam, usually in vacuum, into a keyhole melting depth-to-width ratios up to about thirty to one with minimal distortion. Its hazards are geometric: a **missed joint**, because a beam a millimeter wide must track the seam exactly, and **spiking** in partial-penetration welds - periodic root spikes with cold-shut **voids at their tips**. Radiographing the narrow root demands precise alignment of the beam with the weld plane.

**Electroslag (ESW)** and **electrogas (EGW)** welding fill very thick vertical joints in a single pass between water-cooled copper shoes - ESW by resistance heating of a molten conductive slag bath (no arc once running), EGW as a gas-shielded arc process. Enormous heat input and slow cooling give coarse grain and lower toughness, with risks of **centerline segregation and cracking**, porosity from damp flux, lack of fusion if power or slag depth wanders, and notorious **restart** defects. **Laser beam welds** behave like small electron-beam welds - keyhole porosity and missed-joint concerns, no vacuum required.

sections.caption: Process versus signature discontinuities - the interpreter's suspect list.
rows: Slag (wagon tracks), porosity, hydrogen cracks, hollow bead roots
Elongated slag, flux-moisture porosity, centerline cracking
Lack of fusion (short-arc), wind porosity, whiskers, silica islands
Flux in the wire core (gas optional)
Slag, worm tracks, porosity from voltage or wind
Tungsten inclusions (light spots), porosity, sugared roots, crater cracks
Nugget voids, cracks, expulsion; stuck welds invisible to RT
Missed joint, spiking root voids, keyhole porosity
Centerline cracking, coarse grain, porosity, restart defects

sections.title: Process first, film second
sections.body: Before interpreting, ask what process made the weld. A sharp **light** inclusion in a GTAW root is tungsten; in a SMAW weld it almost certainly is not. A 'slag line' called on a GMAW weld deserves skepticism - there is no flux to make it. Lack of sidewall fusion belongs to short-arc wire welds; centerline cracks belong to deep single-pass beads (SAW, ESW). The WPS is interpretation evidence - use it.

figures.title: Four arc processes and their shielding

interactive.title: Match the Discontinuity to the Process
interactive.intro: Each radiographic finding below carries a clue to the process that made the weld. Sort each one into the process family it points to.

items.label: Sharp white (light) spot, far denser than steel, in a stainless root pass
items.why: Only GTAW uses a tungsten electrode; dense tungsten fragments absorb more of the beam and image light.

items.label: Two parallel dark lines riding the groove faces of a multi-pass field weld - wagon tracks
items.why: Wagon tracks are slag wedged at the bevel toes between passes - a signature of the flux-shielded stick process.

items.label: Smooth, straight dark line along one sidewall of a positional weld made with short-circuiting transfer
items.why: Short-circuit GMAW runs cold; the puddle can freeze against an unmelted sidewall, leaving its classic lack-of-fusion line.

items.label: Hollow bead - elongated porosity along the root centerline of a downhill cellulosic pipeline root
items.why: Cellulosic stick electrodes run downhill on pipeline roots; hollow bead is their signature root discontinuity.

items.label: Oxidized, granular 'sugared' root on a stainless tube weld after the back purge ran out
items.why: GTAW tube work relies on an inert back purge; losing it lets the stainless root oxidize - sugaring.

items.label: Long, uniform slag line beside an automated flat-position vessel seam welded under a granular blanket
items.why: The granular blanket is SAW flux; when SAW traps slag it does so in long, regular lines along the fusion boundaries.

items.label: Worm tracks - elongated gas grooves under the slag of a high-deposition weld made with tubular wire
items.why: Tubular wire carrying its own flux is FCAW; moisture in the core makes the elongated surface gas grooves called worm tracks.

items.label: Scattered porosity that appeared when a gusty crosswind hit a solid-wire gun working outdoors
items.why: GMAW's external gas envelope is easily stripped by wind above roughly five miles per hour - the classic field porosity outbreak.

checkQuestions.question: A sharp, very light (white) spot appears in the root area of a weld. Which process made the weld, and why is the spot light?
options: SMAW - a slag particle reflects radiation
GTAW - a tungsten fragment, denser than steel, absorbed more of the beam so less radiation reached the film
SAW - trapped flux is denser than steel
GMAW - a silica island blocked the beam
checkQuestions.explanation: Only GTAW uses a tungsten electrode; tungsten is far denser than steel, transmits less radiation, and therefore prints lighter than the surrounding weld.

checkQuestions.question: The signature discontinuity of short-circuiting transfer GMAW is:
options: Tungsten inclusions
Wagon-track slag lines
Lack of sidewall fusion (cold lap) from low heat input
Excessive penetration
checkQuestions.explanation: GMAW-S runs cold so the puddle can roll against a sidewall without melting it, freezing into a smooth unfused line along the fusion face - the process's notorious weakness.

checkQuestions.question: Slag inclusions are NOT a credible call on a weld made by which process?
options: SMAW
SAW
FCAW
GMAW with solid wire
checkQuestions.explanation: GMAW shields with gas alone and uses a solid wire - there is no flux to form slag, only small glassy silica islands, so a slag call on solid-wire GMAW deserves a second look.

checkQuestions.question: Partial-penetration electron beam welds are particularly prone to which root condition?
options: Spiking, with cold-shut voids at the tips of the root spikes
Sugaring from lost back purge
Hollow bead
Worm tracks from flux moisture
checkQuestions.explanation: The EB keyhole advances unevenly in partial-penetration work, leaving periodic root spikes whose tips trap voids - a defect unique to high-energy-density keyhole welding.

checkQuestions.question: Why is radiography a poor method for verifying that a resistance spot weld actually fused (i.e., is not a 'stuck' or cold weld)?
options: The sheets are too thin to radiograph
An unfused but tight interface produces almost no change in metal path, so the film records no density difference
The copper electrodes leave permanent shadows
Spot welds are always sound if current flowed
checkQuestions.explanation: RT can show nugget porosity, cracks, and expulsion, but a stuck weld is an intimate unbonded interface with no thickness change along the beam - ultrasonic and mechanical tests verify bond quality.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Film Quality and Manufacturing Processes Course §4.3-4.3.7
AWS welding process literature on arc, resistance, and high-energy-density processes (general reference)
Codes such as ASME Section V and B31.3 govern the radiographic acceptance of these welds (general reference)
